title: The Separated Women
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Separated Women
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Go lying through the land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For they have plenty dresses,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And money, too, in hand;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They married brutes and drunkards
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And blackguards ן¿½frightful lowן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But why are they so eager
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For all the world to know?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shamed and ill-used woman
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who really longs to die,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She slaves at home in silence
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hides her poor black eye!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She lives a life of terror
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eased off at times in woe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But why is she so frightened
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That any one might know?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Separated Woman
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She rushes to the court,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sad, shabby and pathetic,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or flaunting or distraught;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The real wronged wife would rather
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lose both eyes and her hair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She swears a lie to save him
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he is taken there.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Separated Woman
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She mostly goes the same,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bag-woman, sham-nurse, ן¿½prettyן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or on her husbandן¿½s name;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The real loafed-on woman,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With courage almost grim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Goes outן¿½ and takes in washing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To keep the kids, and him.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Separated Woman,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I knew her course so well:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The Stageן¿½, then first-class barmaid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then third-class bar, and hell:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½hellן¿½ means all things vicious
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That prey upon the town
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (She wishes her poor husband
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had sometimes knocked her down).

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Masseur and manicurist,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or anything by chance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They vilify their husbands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And draw the maintenance.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sham artists, ן¿½music teachersן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! they are flinty nuts!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their friends are man-shaped crawlers
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And lower than the dust.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The separated ן¿½Monstersן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are missing from the tale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They seem to have cleared out, or,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps they are in gaol.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The separated husband
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is heard of here and there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A mild and decent citizen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And mostly bowed with care.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Separated Women,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When upset in the track,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are often very eager
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To take the ן¿½Monsterן¿½ back.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½ve moved all hell to crush him
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, startled, find too late
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Monsterן¿½s grown content with
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The separated state.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
